summary: Stellar Help→Slip→Franklin sequence, tight jams & first‑time gems
blurb: The show shines with an all‑time killer Help → Slip → Franklin's Tower and a fiery Crazy Fingers jam. Scarlet Begonias and the debut of The Wheel add historic weight, while Keith’s keyboards sit front‑and‑center. Overall a high‑energy performance worth revisiting.
review: Portland’s June 3, 1976 gig is one of those classic ’76 outings that still feels fresh. From the opening “Might As Well” through a bright set that rolls into a standout second half, the band delivers a mix of new material and beloved staples with remarkable chemistry. The Help → Slip → Franklin’s Tower sequence is repeatedly called an all‑time killer – Garcia’s tone is described as perfect, his solo work “outstanding,” and Keith’s piano/organ sits forward in the mix, adding lush texture. Highlights like a monster Crazy Fingers jam, a crisp Scarlet Begonias, and the first live renditions of The Wheel and Samson & Delilah give the night historic gravitas. Drummers Bill and Mickey lock into a solid groove, with a tasty Let‑It‑Grow drum segment and a short sweet solo that punctuates the set. While a few recordings note minor tuning pauses or a slightly shaky Lazy Lightning/Supplication segue, the consensus is clear: this show captures the band at a creative peak, making it a must‑listen for any Deadhead.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Strong positive sentiment across all recordings: multiple reviewers call the Help→Slip→Franklin sequence "all‑time killer" and praise Garcia's tone, Keith's keyboards, and the overall tightness. Only minor negatives (tuning pauses, a single poor Lightning/Supplication note) appear, so a robust +1.6 adjustment reflects the overwhelmingly enthusiastic consensus.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with crystal‑clear mix, balanced drums and prominent keyboards; minimal technical issues and consistently praised as the most faithful representation of the performance.

key_highlights: All‑time killer Help → Slip → Franklin's Tower sequence
Fiery Crazy Fingers jam and tight Scarlet Begonias
First live renditions of The Wheel and Samson & Delilah
Keith Godchaux’s forward, beautiful keyboard work

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers flawless tone and soaring solos, his guitar work on the Help‑Slip‑Franklin sequence and the gritty Dancin’ solo is repeatedly praised as outstanding.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a rock‑solid bass foundation with “unreal licks” that lock the band tightly together throughout the set.
band_performance.Brent: Keith Godchaux’s piano and organ are forward in the mix, offering beautiful, full‑genius playing that lifts the jams, especially on Scarlet Begonias and the second‑set improvisations.
band_performance.Mickey: Mickey Hart’s drumming is audible and drives the grooves, contributing a tasty segment within the Let It Grow sandwich and solidifying the band’s rhythmic pulse.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann’s steady groove pairs perfectly with Mickey, delivering a crisp drum solo that fans describe as short and sweet.
